behaviors.description: A cell containing =SUM(A1:A10) evaluates to the correct sum of the values in cells A1 through A10.
behaviors.justification: Testing the behavior as stated requires wiring together multiple components: the cell grid, formula parser, range resolver, and SUM function. While the SUM algorithm itself can be unit-tested with mock arrays, the real failure boundary is whether the spreadsheet correctly parses the formula, resolves the range, and computes the sum end-to-end. A pure mock of cell values would not catch issues in range parsing (e.g., off-by-one errors, missing cells, or incorrect inclusive/exclusive bounds).
behaviors.test_description: Create a spreadsheet with cells A1 through A10 containing known numerical values (positive, negative, zero, floats). Add a formula =SUM(A1:A10) to another cell. Evaluate and verify the result matches the expected sum. Test edge cases: empty cells within the range, non-numeric cells (should be ignored or error), very large/small numbers, and floating-point precision.

behaviors.description: A cell referencing another cell that contains a formula (e.g., =B1+1 where B1 is =A1*2) produces the correct cascaded result.
behaviors.justification: This behavior tests the dependency resolution and evaluation order of the formula engine. While each individual function (addition, multiplication) is unit-testable, the real failure would be incorrect topological ordering of evaluations or missing intermediate recalculations. Mocking individual cell evaluations would mask order-dependent bugs. The failure boundary requires testing the interaction between the dependency graph and the evaluation engine.
behaviors.test_description: Create cells A1 (value), B1 (formula =A1*2), and C1 (formula =B1+1). Verify C1 evaluates to (A1*2)+1. Test with longer chains (3+ levels). Test that changing A1 triggers recalculation of B1 and C1 in correct order (B1 before C1). Verify intermediate values are correct at each step.

behaviors.description: The engine detects a circular dependency (A1 references B1, B1 references A1) and returns a circular reference error instead of looping.
behaviors.justification: While cycle detection in an abstract graph can be unit-tested, the real failure is whether the formula engine correctly parses formulas, builds the dependency graph, detects cycles, and returns an error instead of hanging. Mocking the graph construction or evaluation would miss failures in formula parsing, incomplete graph building, or error-handling logic. The test must exercise the full pipeline: formula → dependency graph → cycle detection.
behaviors.test_description: Create cells A1 and B1 with circular references: A1 contains =B1, B1 contains =A1. Attempt to evaluate A1. Verify a circular reference error (#CIRC or equivalent) is returned instead of infinite recursion or timeout. Test longer cycles (A→B→C→A). Verify that other cells in the spreadsheet remain evaluable and unaffected.

behaviors.description: VLOOKUP with an exact match returns the correct value from the lookup table, and returns an error when the key is not found.
behaviors.justification: VLOOKUP must search a multi-cell range (the lookup table) and return a value from a specific column. While the search algorithm can be unit-tested with mock arrays, the real failure is whether the engine correctly resolves the range reference, handles cell types, and navigates rows/columns in the actual spreadsheet structure. Mocking the lookup table would miss issues with range resolution, type coercion, and boundary handling in the cell grid.
behaviors.test_description: Create a lookup table in cells A1:B5 (keys in column A, values in column B). Add formula =VLOOKUP("key2", A1:B5, 2, FALSE). Verify it returns the correct value. Test with exact and inexact keys. Test with string and numeric keys. Test that missing keys return #N/A error. Test range boundary conditions and empty cells in the table.

behaviors.description: The IF function correctly handles nested conditions (e.g., =IF(A1>10, IF(A1>20, "high", "medium"), "low")).
behaviors.justification: Although IF is primarily a function-level concern, testing the stated behavior requires the formula parser and cell value lookup to work correctly alongside the IF logic. The real failure would be in incorrect short-circuit evaluation, type coercion, or missing branch evaluation. While the IF algorithm itself is unit-testable, evaluating a nested IF formula in a spreadsheet requires the full integration pipeline.
behaviors.test_description: Create cell A1 with numeric values (5, 15, 25). For each value, evaluate nested IF formula and verify correct branch is returned: A1=5 → "low", A1=15 → "medium", A1=25 → "high". Test with boolean conditions, string comparisons, and formulas in branches. Verify all branches evaluate and the correct one is returned.

behaviors.description: CONCATENATE joins cell values with the correct separator and handles empty cells by skipping them rather than inserting blank strings.
behaviors.justification: CONCATENATE behavior depends on how the engine represents empty cells and passes them to the function. The real failure is whether empty cells are correctly identified and excluded. While testing the concatenation algorithm with mock empty values is unit-testable, the integration boundary is how cells are resolved and their empty-state is determined in the spreadsheet context.
behaviors.test_description: Create cells with text values and empty cells. Evaluate =CONCATENATE(A1, "-", B1, "-", C1) where B1 is empty. Verify result is "value1-value3" (skipping empty B1) not "value1--value3". Test with different separators, multiple empty cells, and various empty representations (null, blank string, etc.).

behaviors.description: A formula referencing a cell in a different sheet (e.g., =Sheet2!A1+1) resolves correctly when the referenced sheet exists.
behaviors.justification: Cross-sheet references require the workbook to contain multiple sheets and the formula evaluator to correctly navigate between them. The real failure is whether sheet names are resolved and the correct cell is found in another sheet. This cannot be tested without wiring multiple sheet objects into a workbook structure.
behaviors.test_description: Create a workbook with Sheet1 and Sheet2. Add data to Sheet2!A1. In Sheet1, add formula =Sheet2!A1+1. Evaluate and verify correct result. Test with invalid sheet names (should error with #NAME? or similar). Test with chains of cross-sheet references. Test that sheet name case-sensitivity is handled correctly.

behaviors.description: When a referenced cell's value changes, all dependent formulas are recalculated in the correct topological order.
behaviors.justification: This tests the recalculation pipeline: dependency tracking, topological sorting, and update propagation. Mocking individual cell evaluations would pass even if the order is wrong or some dependents are skipped. The real failure is in the interaction between the dependency graph, the change notification system, and the evaluation engine. Cannot be caught without testing the full recalculation mechanism.
behaviors.test_description: Set up cells: A1=5, B1=A1*2, C1=B1+1, D1=A1+C1. Verify initial values. Change A1 to 10. Verify B1, C1, D1 are all recalculated with new values in correct order (B1→C1→D1). Test with large dependency chains (10+ levels). Test with cells having multiple dependencies. Verify that changing B1 does not trigger recalculation of A1 (one-way dependencies).

behaviors.description: The undo operation after editing a formula restores both the formula text and all previously computed dependent values.
behaviors.justification: Undo requires capturing the pre-edit state (formula + all dependent values), executing the edit, recalculating dependents, and then restoring the old state. Mocking the undo stack would miss failures in state capture, incomplete value restoration, or dependency recalculation. The real failure is in the interaction between editing, calculation, and undo—all three must work together.
behaviors.test_description: Create cells with dependent formulas. Edit a formula (e.g., =A1*2 → =A1*3). Verify dependents are recalculated with new values. Undo the edit. Verify formula is restored to original text and all dependent values revert to pre-edit state. Test multiple levels of dependencies. Test undo/redo sequences. Test undoing edits to cells with multiple dependents.

behaviors.description: Saving a workbook to disk and loading it back produces identical cell values and formulas—no data loss or precision change in floating-point results.
behaviors.justification: This behavior depends on real file I/O: serialization to a binary format on disk and deserialization. Mocking file I/O would not catch real failures like byte-order issues, floating-point precision loss, encoding errors, or file corruption. The failure boundary is the actual file system—the test must write to and read from actual disk.
behaviors.test_description: Create a workbook with various cell types: formulas, numeric values with high precision (10+ decimal places), cross-sheet references, and circular-reference errors. Save to binary file. Load into a new workbook object. Verify all formulas are identical, all numeric values match exactly (bit-for-bit for floats), and dependent calculations still work correctly. Test edge cases: NaN, Infinity, very large exponents, very small denormalized floats.
